anderson-promoters.name: Anderson promoters
anderson-promoters.description: The Anderson constitutive promoter library (BBa_J231xx): a family of short (35 bp) sigma-70 promoters that share one -35/-10 scaffold and differ at only a few positions, giving a graded series of constitutive strengths from the strong references (J23100, J23119) down to weak members. Widely used as a calibrated set of constitutive expression levels in bacteria.

references.title: Measuring the activity of BioBrick promoters using an in vivo reference standard
references.authors: Kelly JR, Rubin AJ, Davis JH, et al.

phage-att-sites.name: Phage attachment sites for chromosomal integration
phage-att-sites.description: Phage-borne attP attachment sites from phylogenetically distinct, mutually orthogonal site-specific recombination systems, each recognized by its own dedicated integrase and not cross-reacting with the others: phiC31 attP (Streptomyces phage phiC31; a large serine integrase that needs no host factors and gives highly directional integration), and the tyrosine-integrase attP sites of coliphages lambda, 186 (a P2-family system that also uses the Apl directionality factor), phi80, and HK022 (which use the host integration host factor IHF, and an excisionase for the reverse reaction). Each attP is the phage half of an attP x attB recombination pair; the cognate bacterial attB sites (in the host chromosome, or a pseudo-attB in non-native hosts for phiC31) are being added. These orthogonal sites are widely used as landing pads for stable, single-copy genomic integration.

affinity-tags.name: Affinity purification tags
affinity-tags.description: Peptide and protein tags that give a recombinant protein a defined, reversible binding handle for single-step affinity capture. The set spans the small metal-chelation peptide 6xHis (IMAC), the streptavidin/Strep-Tactin–binding Strep-tag II and Twin-Strep-tag, the FLAG octapeptide (anti-FLAG resin), the S-protein–binding S-tag, the calmodulin-binding peptide (CBP) and streptavidin-binding peptide (SBP) used in tandem-affinity workflows, and the larger fusion-protein handles GST (glutathione resin) and MBP (amylose resin), which double as solubility partners. Each is captured on an immobilised cognate ligand and released under mild, tag-specific conditions (imidazole, desthiobiotin/biotin, competing peptide, glutathione, maltose, or EGTA).

references.title: Overview of tag protein fusions: from molecular and biochemical fundamentals to commercial systems
Overview of affinity tags for protein purification
references.authors: Kimple ME, Brill AL, Pasker RL

epitope-tags.name: Epitope / detection tags
epitope-tags.description: Short linear peptide epitopes recognised by a well-characterised monoclonal antibody or nanobody, used to detect, localise, or immunoprecipitate a tagged protein without a native antibody. The collection includes the classical antibody epitopes HA, Myc, FLAG, T7, V5 and VSV-G, the bacteriophage-derived E-tag, and the modern high-affinity nanobody epitopes ALFA-tag, Spot-tag and BC2-tag, several of which support super-resolution imaging through small, single-domain binders with low linkage error. Detection is via the cognate antibody/nanobody; many double as affinity handles on an immobilised binder.

references.title: Overview of tag protein fusions: from molecular and biochemical fundamentals to commercial systems

solubility-tags.name: Solubility-enhancing fusion partners
solubility-tags.description: Larger protein fusion partners that promote the soluble, correctly folded expression of an otherwise aggregation-prone passenger, typically as an N-terminal fusion later removed by a site-specific protease. The set comprises the E. coli proteins MBP (maltose-binding protein), thioredoxin (Trx), and NusA, the Schistosoma GST, and the ubiquitin-like SUMO (yeast Smt3), whose cognate protease Ulp1 cleaves at the folded SUMO domain to release a native N-terminus. Several of these (MBP, GST) are also affinity handles.

references.title: Overview of tag protein fusions: from molecular and biochemical fundamentals to commercial systems

protease-cleavage-sites.name: Protease cleavage sites for tag removal
protease-cleavage-sites.description: Short recognition sequences engineered into the linker between an affinity/solubility tag and the protein of interest so the tag can be removed by a sequence-specific protease after purification, plus the SUMO-specific protease Ulp1. The cleavage sites include TEV (ENLYFQG), HRV 3C/PreScission (LEVLFQGP), thrombin (LVPRGS), enterokinase (DDDDK) and Factor Xa (IEGR); they differ in specificity, the residual N-terminal scar, and off-target propensity. Ulp1 instead recognises the folded SUMO domain rather than a linear motif and cleaves scarlessly.

references.title: An overview of enzymatic reagents for the removal of affinity tags
A critical review of the methods for cleavage of fusion proteins with thrombin and factor Xa
references.authors: Jenny RJ, Mann KG, Lundblad RL

conjugation-tags.name: Conjugation & self-labeling systems
conjugation-tags.description: Tags that form a covalent or enzyme-mediated link to a partner, for irreversible bioconjugation, site-specific labelling, or modular protein assembly. The collection covers the spontaneous isopeptide 'superglue' pairs SpyTag/SpyCatcher (and the faster SpyTag002/003) and the orthogonal SnoopTag/SnoopCatcher; the enzymatic systems AviTag (site-specifically biotinylated by the biotin ligase BirA) and the sortase-A LPETG motif (transpeptidation onto an N-terminal oligoglycine); and the self-labeling protein tag SNAP-tag, which covalently captures O6-benzylguanine–linked probes. These parts are deployed as cognate pairs (tag + catcher/enzyme/substrate).
conjugation-tags.source: Covalent / enzymatic protein conjugation & self-labeling systems
